levels.title: Advanced pico-ioc Patterns
levels.description: Master configuration, factories, AOP interceptors, lifecycle hooks, and testing with pico-ioc.

levels.title: @configured — Type-Safe Settings
levels.description:
Type-Safe Configuration with @configured

The @configured decorator binds a Python dataclass to a configuration source. At container startup, pico-ioc automatically populates the fields from the config tree.

You specify a prefix to select the subtree and mapping="tree" to recursively map nested dicts onto the dataclass fields.

In main.py, we use DictSource to provide an in-memory config dict and pass it via configuration() to init(). The container then creates a fully populated AppSettings instance.

Your task: add the @configured(prefix="app", mapping="tree") decorator to the AppSettings dataclass in app.py.

files.name: app.py
files.content:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pico_ioc import configured

# 👈 Add @configured(prefix="app", mapping="tree") here
@dataclass
class AppSettings:
    name: str = "default"
    debug: bool = False

files.name: main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
from pico_ioc import configuration, DictSource
import app

config = configuration(DictSource({"app": {"name": "MyApp", "debug": True}}))
container = pico.init(modules=[app], config=config)

settings = container.get(app.AppSettings)
print(f"App name: {settings.name}")
print(f"Debug mode: {settings.debug}")

hints: The @configured decorator goes right above @dataclass, and it takes keyword arguments.
You need two arguments: prefix tells pico-ioc where to look in the config tree, and mapping tells it how to bind values.

levels.title: @factory and @provides
levels.description:
Factories for Complex Object Creation

Sometimes you need more control over how an object is created than a simple constructor allows. The @factory decorator marks a class whose methods produce components. Each producer method is decorated with @provides.

The factory class itself is managed by the container, so its methods can receive injected dependencies. The @provides(SomeType) decorator tells pico-ioc that the method's return value should be registered as the provider for SomeType.

In app.py, the InfraFactory class is already decorated with @factory. Your task: add @provides(DatabaseClient) to the build_db method so the container knows how to create a DatabaseClient.

files.name: app.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import app

container = pico.init(modules=[app])

db = container.get(app.DatabaseClient)
print(db.query("SELECT * FROM users"))

hints: The @provides decorator goes above the method definition inside the factory class.
You need to tell @provides which type this method produces by passing the class as an argument.
Add @provides(DatabaseClient) right above the build_db method.

levels.title: Method Interceptors (AOP)
levels.description:
Aspect-Oriented Programming with Interceptors

Interceptors let you wrap cross-cutting logic (logging, timing, validation) around method calls without modifying the method itself. An interceptor is a @component that implements the invoke(self, ctx, call_next) method.

The @intercepted_by(InterceptorClass) decorator is placed on the method you want to intercept. The interceptor receives a MethodCtx object with details about the call and a call_next function to proceed to the actual method.

In app.py, the LoggingInterceptor is ready. Your task: add @intercepted_by(LoggingInterceptor) to the add method of Calculator.

files.name: app.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import app

container = pico.init(modules=[app])

calc = container.get(app.Calculator)
result = calc.add(2, 3)
print(f"Result: {result}")

hints: The @intercepted_by decorator goes on the method that should be intercepted, not on the class.
Pass the interceptor class (not an instance) as the argument to @intercepted_by.
Add @intercepted_by(LoggingInterceptor) directly above the add method.

levels.title: Lifecycle: @configure and @cleanup
levels.description:
Post-Construction and Shutdown Hooks

Components often need to perform setup after construction or cleanup before shutdown. The @configure decorator marks a method that runs right after the component is fully created and injected. The @cleanup decorator marks a method that runs when container.shutdown() is called.

This is useful for initializing connection pools, warming caches, or releasing resources gracefully.

In app.py, the ConnectionPool has a setup method that should run at startup and a close method that runs at shutdown. The @cleanup is already in place. Your task: add the @configure decorator to the setup method.

files.name: app.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import app

container = pico.init(modules=[app])

pool = container.get(app.ConnectionPool)
print(f"Pool status: {pool.status()}")

container.shutdown()
print(f"Pool status after shutdown: {pool.status()}")

hints: The @configure decorator marks a method to run automatically after the component is created.
It goes directly above the method definition, just like @cleanup does for the close method.
Add @configure on the line above def setup(self):.

levels.title: Testing with Overrides
levels.description:
Replacing Components for Testing

The overrides parameter in pico.init() lets you swap out real components with mocks or fakes. This is essential for unit testing: you can replace a payment gateway, a database, or any dependency without changing your production code.

The overrides dict maps a type (the class to replace) to an instance or callable. When the container encounters a dependency on that type, it uses your override instead.

In test_main.py, we want to test OrderService with a mock gateway. The override syntax is wrong. Your task: fix the overrides dict so that PaymentGateway is replaced by the mock_gw instance.

files.name: app.py
test_main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
import app

class MockGateway:
    def charge(self, amount: float) -> str:
        return f"Mock charge: ${amount}"

def test_order_with_mock():
    mock_gw = MockGateway()

    # 👈 Fix the overrides syntax below
    container = pico.init(modules=[app], overrides={"PaymentGateway": mock_gw})

    order_svc = container.get(app.OrderService)
    result = order_svc.place_order(99.99)
    print(result)
    assert "Mock charge" in result

hints: The overrides dict key should be a type, not a string. Think about what the container uses to look up the dependency.
OrderService depends on PaymentGateway via its type hint. The override key must match that type.
Change the key from the string "PaymentGateway" to the actual class: app.PaymentGateway.

solution.test_main.py:
import pico_ioc as pico
import app

class MockGateway:
    def charge(self, amount: float) -> str:
        return f"Mock charge: ${amount}"

def test_order_with_mock():
    mock_gw = MockGateway()

    # 👈 Fix the overrides syntax below
    container = pico.init(modules=[app], overrides={app.PaymentGateway: mock_gw})

    order_svc = container.get(app.OrderService)
    result = order_svc.place_order(99.99)
    print(result)
    assert "Mock charge" in result

levels.title: Putting It All Together
levels.description:
Combining Advanced Patterns

Let's wire up a mini-application that uses several advanced features at once: @configured for settings, @component for services with dependency injection, @intercepted_by for logging, and overrides for testing.

You have five files. Some decorators are missing. Your task is to add the missing decorators so that everything works together:

  • config.py — needs @configured
  • services.py — needs @component on OrderService
  • interceptors.py — needs @intercepted_by wired in services.py
  • test_main.py — already complete

files.name: config.py
files.content:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pico_ioc import configured

# 👈 Add @configured(prefix="store", mapping="tree")
@dataclass
class StoreSettings:
    name: str = "DefaultStore"
    tax_rate: float = 0.1

files.name: interceptors.py
services.py
main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
from pico_ioc import configuration, DictSource
import config
import interceptors
import services

cfg = configuration(DictSource({"store": {"name": "PyShop", "tax_rate": 0.08}}))
container = pico.init(modules=[config, interceptors, services], config=cfg)

order_svc = container.get(services.OrderService)
print(order_svc.place_order(100.0))

files.name: test_main.py
files.content:
import pico_ioc as pico
from pico_ioc import configuration, DictSource
import config
import interceptors
import services

class MockGateway:
    def charge(self, amount: float) -> str:
        return f"MockCharge ${amount:.2f}"

def test_order_with_override():
    cfg = configuration(DictSource({"store": {"name": "TestShop", "tax_rate": 0.0}}))
    mock_gw = MockGateway()
    container = pico.init(
        modules=[config, interceptors, services],
        config=cfg,
        overrides={services.PaymentGateway: mock_gw}
    )

    order_svc = container.get(services.OrderService)
    result = order_svc.place_order(50.0)
    print(result)
    assert "TestShop" in result
    assert "MockCharge $50.00" in result

hints: There are three missing decorators across two files. Start with config.py since services depend on StoreSettings.
In config.py, add @configured(prefix="store", mapping="tree") above @dataclass. In services.py, add @component above class OrderService.
Finally, in services.py, add @intercepted_by(AuditInterceptor) above the place_order method.
